messages.content:
A statistician walks into a room and wants to
know how likely it is that someone in the room shares a birthday with HER
specifically (not with each other — with her).

Assume 365 equally likely birthdays, ignore leap years, and that she knows
her own birthday. What is the minimum number of OTHER people that must be in
the room so that the probability at least one of them shares HER birthday
exceeds 50%?

Think carefully about exactly which event is being asked about.


When you are finished, output your final answer on its own line, as the last line of your response, in the exact format:
ANSWER: <your answer>

text:
This is the "same birthday as me" variant of the birthday problem, not the classic pairwise version.

With n other people, the probability that none of them shares her birthday is (364/365)^n. We need:

1 − (364/365)^n > 0.5, i.e. (364/365)^n < 0.5

Taking logs: n > ln(2) / ln(365/364) ≈ 0.693147 / 0.0027435 ≈ 252.65

So n = 253. Checking: (364/365)^252 ≈ 0.5008 (probability of a match ≈ 49.9%, not enough), while (364/365)^253 ≈ 0.4995, giving a match probability ≈ 50.05% > 50%.

Notably, 253 is much larger than the famous "23" answer, because that classic result counts matches among *any pair* in the room, whereas here every comparison must involve her specific birthday.

ANSWER: 253
